news_summary: Significant progress in human rights is evident as Lebanon moves closer to abolishing the death penalty, France legalizes assisted dying for adults, peacekeepers enhance support for returning families in south Lebanon, and Gaza farmers strive to rebuild amid efforts to address regional violence.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès significatifs en matière de droits de l'homme sont manifestes : le Liban se rapproche de l'abolition de la peine de mort, la France légalise l'aide à mourir pour les adultes, les forces de maintien de la paix renforcent leur soutien aux familles qui rentrent chez elles dans le sud du Liban, et les agriculteurs de Gaza s'efforcent de reconstruire leurs exploitations alors que des efforts sont déployés pour lutter contre la violence dans la région.
news_summary_es: Se observan avances significativos en materia de derechos humanos a medida que el Líbano se acerca a la abolición de la pena de muerte, Francia legaliza la muerte asistida para adultos, las fuerzas de mantenimiento de la paz refuerzan el apoyo a las familias que regresan al sur del Líbano y los agricultores de Gaza se esfuerzan por reconstruir sus vidas en medio de los esfuerzos por hacer frente a la violencia regional.

articles.title: Lebanon: Abolishing Death Penalty Within Reach
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Lawmakers at the parliament building in Beirut, Lebanon, June 14, 2023. 
                    © 2023 Hassan Ammar/AP Photo 
          
    


  
(Beirut) – Members of three parliamentary committees in Lebanon jointly approved a draft law abolishing the death penalty on July 9, 2026, putting Lebanon on the precipice of becoming the first country in the Middle East region to formally end capital punishment, Human Rights Watch said today. The draft law requires approval by the parliament’s general assembly to become law. Lebanese lawmakers meeting on July 15 and 16 to discuss and vote on this draft law, among others, should pass the law and cement this monumental advance for human rights and criminal justice in Lebanon. “Despite an unofficial moratorium on the use of the death penalty for the past two decades, Lebanon needs to go a step further and declare a firm break from this cruel practice,” said Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Parliament should ensure that this law is enacted and the death penalty is removed from all provisions of Lebanese law.”Lebanon has an unofficial moratorium on the death penalty and has not carried out an execution since 2004. In 2020, 2022, and 2024, Lebanon voted in favor of a United Nations General Assembly resolution calling upon “all states to establish a moratorium on executions, with a view towards abolishing the death penalty.” The Lebanese Association for Civil Rights (LACR), which prepared the draft law, estimates that about 84 prisoners are facing death sentences in Lebanon. Human Rights Watch spoke to Michel Moussa, the head of the Parliamentary Human Rights Committee; Halime Kaakour, one of seven members of parliament who submitted the draft law to parliament; and Ogarit Younan, the head of the LACR and founder of the National Campaign to Abolish the Death Penalty in Lebanon, in addition to an adviser to the justice minister. Human Rights Watch also reviewed the version of the draft law originally submitted to parliament, the versions approved by the Parliamentary Human Rights Committee and the Administration and Justice Committee, the latest version of the law approved by three parliamentary committees in a joint session, and the government’s advisory opinion on the draft law.In its latest form, the draft law abolishes the death penalty “wherever it is mentioned under Lebanese laws” and replaces it with a newly created penalty, “life imprisonment with aggravated hard labor.” It amends article 37 of the Penal Code to read that way and annuls article 43 of Lebanon’s Penal Code and articles 420 to 424 of Lebanon’s Code of Criminal Procedure, which set out the procedures for carrying out death sentences. In its analysis of the law, Lebanese rights group Legal Agenda said that “this amounts to the creation of a new criminal penalty, […] even though this innovation has no practical effect,” given that Lebanon does not enforce hard labor provisions.Under the UN Standard Minimum Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners, “Prison labour must not be of an afflictive nature.” Seven members of parliament had endorsed the version of the law drafted by the National Campaign to Abolish the Death Penalty in Lebanon and submitted it to parliament on October 7, 2025, according to Younan and a copy of the submitted draft law Human Rights Watch reviewed. Younan said that parliament’s General Secretariat subsequently sent the law to the cabinet, seeking an advisory opinion.The cabinet discussed the draft law during a November 20 session and endorsed it, noting the need to also annul article 43 of the penal code, based on a copy of the government’s advisory opinion seen by Human Rights Watch. The draft law was subsequently amended and reviewed by Lebanon’s Parliamentary Human Rights Committee, the Administration and Justice Committee, and by a joint session of three parliamentary committees, also including the Communications Committee. After its approval by the committees on July 9, it was sent to parliament’s General Assembly to be discussed and voted on during the July 15 and 16 sessions, based on its agenda.“In Lebanon, people are dying every day because of the war,” Younan said. “I would have hoped that this law would pass at a different time, so that we could celebrate, but it is happening in a difficult situation. Congratulations to Lebanon. We can finally consider it one of the countries that has abolished the death penalty. This is no small achievement […] Our fight continues, this law should be developed and amended to remove these insulting provisions, including on aggravated hard labor.”Lebanon’s move to abolish the death penalty comes amid backsliding on the issue in neighboring countries. On March 30, the Israeli Knesset passed a discriminatory bill expanding use of the death penalty, whose wording makes clear that it would be primarily, if not exclusively, applied to Palestinians. On June 21, 2026, Jordanian authorities executed six men by hanging in its first executions since 2017. Human Rights Watch opposes capital punishment in all countries and under all circumstances. Capital punishment is unique in its cruelty and finality and is plagued with arbitrariness, prejudice, and error. Most countries have abolished the practice outright, while dozens have adopted a de facto moratorium. Human Rights Watch found in 2017 that Lebanon’s military courts, which have broad jurisdiction over civilians and retain the death penalty, do not guarantee due process rights. In 2024, Lebanon’s military court sentenced two individuals to death, according to Together Against the Death Penalty (Ensemble contre la peine de mort, ECPM). Those who have stood trial in military court describe the use of confessions extracted under torture, decisions issued without an explanation, seemingly arbitrary sentences, and a limited ability to appeal.In addition to passing a law abolishing the death penalty, Lebanon should remove civilians from the jurisdiction of military courts and consider ratifying the Second Optional Protocol to the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, aiming at the abolition of the death penalty. Lebanon’s parliament should further remove hard labor as an additional form of punishment under Lebanon’s law, Human Rights Watch said.“With the adoption of this law, Lebanon would finally be able join the majority of countries around the world that have recognized the death penalty as a cruel and arbitrary sentence that should never be imposed,” Kaiss said. “It is now up to members of parliament to make that happen.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Lawmakers at the parliament building in Beirut, Lebanon, June 14, 2023. © 2023 Hassan Ammar/AP Photo

(Beirut) – Members of three parliamentary committees in Lebanon jointly approved a draft law abolishing the death penalty on July 9, 2026, putting Lebanon on the precipice of becoming the first country in the Middle East region to formally end capital punishment, Human Rights Watch said today.

The draft law requires approval by the parliament’s general assembly to become law. Lebanese lawmakers meeting on July 15 and 16 to discuss and vote on this draft law, among others, should pass the law and cement this monumental advance for human rights and criminal justice in Lebanon.

“Despite an unofficial moratorium on the use of the death penalty for the past two decades, Lebanon needs to go a step further and declare a firm break from this cruel practice,” said Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Parliament should ensure that this law is enacted and the death penalty is removed from all provisions of Lebanese law.”

Lebanon has an unofficial moratorium on the death penalty and has not carried out an execution since 2004. In 2020, 2022, and 2024, Lebanon voted in favor of a United Nations General Assembly resolution calling upon “all states to establish a moratorium on executions, with a view towards abolishing the death penalty.” The Lebanese Association for Civil Rights (LACR), which prepared the draft law, estimates that about 84 prisoners are facing death sentences in Lebanon.

Human Rights Watch spoke to Michel Moussa, the head of the Parliamentary Human Rights Committee; Halime Kaakour, one of seven members of parliament who submitted the draft law to parliament; and Ogarit Younan, the head of the LACR and founder of the National Campaign to Abolish the Death Penalty in Lebanon, in addition to an adviser to the justice minister. Human Rights Watch also reviewed the version of the draft law originally submitted to parliament, the versions approved by the Parliamentary Human Rights Committee and the Administration and Justice Committee, the latest version of the law approved by three parliamentary committees in a joint session, and the government’s advisory opinion on the draft law.

In its latest form, the draft law abolishes the death penalty “wherever it is mentioned under Lebanese laws” and replaces it with a newly created penalty, “life imprisonment with aggravated hard labor.” It amends article 37 of the Penal Code to read that way and annuls article 43 of Lebanon’s Penal Code and articles 420 to 424 of Lebanon’s Code of Criminal Procedure, which set out the procedures for carrying out death sentences. In its analysis of the law, Lebanese rights group Legal Agenda said that “this amounts to the creation of a new criminal penalty, […] even though this innovation has no practical effect,” given that Lebanon does not enforce hard labor provisions.

Under the UN Standard Minimum Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners, “Prison labour must not be of an afflictive nature.”

Seven members of parliament had endorsed the version of the law drafted by the National Campaign to Abolish the Death Penalty in Lebanon and submitted it to parliament on October 7, 2025, according to Younan and a copy of the submitted draft law Human Rights Watch reviewed. Younan said that parliament’s General Secretariat subsequently sent the law to the cabinet, seeking an advisory opinion.

The cabinet discussed the draft law during a November 20 session and endorsed it, noting the need to also annul article 43 of the penal code, based on a copy of the government’s advisory opinion seen by Human Rights Watch.

The draft law was subsequently amended and reviewed by Lebanon’s Parliamentary Human Rights Committee, the Administration and Justice Committee, and by a joint session of three parliamentary committees, also including the Communications Committee. After its approval by the committees on July 9, it was sent to parliament’s General Assembly to be discussed and voted on during the July 15 and 16 sessions, based on its agenda.

“In Lebanon, people are dying every day because of the war,” Younan said. “I would have hoped that this law would pass at a different time, so that we could celebrate, but it is happening in a difficult situation. Congratulations to Lebanon. We can finally consider it one of the countries that has abolished the death penalty. This is no small achievement […] Our fight continues, this law should be developed and amended to remove these insulting provisions, including on aggravated hard labor.”

Lebanon’s move to abolish the death penalty comes amid backsliding on the issue in neighboring countries. On March 30, the Israeli Knesset passed a discriminatory bill expanding use of the death penalty, whose wording makes clear that it would be primarily, if not exclusively, applied to Palestinians. On June 21, 2026, Jordanian authorities executed six men by hanging in its first executions since 2017.

Human Rights Watch opposes capital punishment in all countries and under all circumstances. Capital punishment is unique in its cruelty and finality and is plagued with arbitrariness, prejudice, and error. Most countries have abolished the practice outright, while dozens have adopted a de facto moratorium.

Human Rights Watch found in 2017 that Lebanon’s military courts, which have broad jurisdiction over civilians and retain the death penalty, do not guarantee due process rights. In 2024, Lebanon’s military court sentenced two individuals to death, according to Together Against the Death Penalty (Ensemble contre la peine de mort, ECPM). Those who have stood trial in military court describe the use of confessions extracted under torture, decisions issued without an explanation, seemingly arbitrary sentences, and a limited ability to appeal.

In addition to passing a law abolishing the death penalty, Lebanon should remove civilians from the jurisdiction of military courts and consider ratifying the Second Optional Protocol to the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, aiming at the abolition of the death penalty. Lebanon’s parliament should further remove hard labor as an additional form of punishment under Lebanon’s law, Human Rights Watch said.

“With the adoption of this law, Lebanon would finally be able join the majority of countries around the world that have recognized the death penalty as a cruel and arbitrary sentence that should never be imposed,” Kaiss said. “It is now up to members of parliament to make that happen.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant legislative advance toward abolishing the death penalty in Lebanon, which would make it the first Middle Eastern country to do so. This represents a meaningful human rights milestone with broad societal impact, supported by detailed context on the legislative process, historical background, and regional significance.
articles.personality_title: Lebanon moves closer to abolishing the death penalty
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lebanon has not carried out any executions since 2004, and it currently has an unofficial pause on the death penalty. For years, Lebanese lawmakers and human rights groups have worked to formally end capital punishment in the country.

**What happened** – On July 9, 2026, three parliamentary committees in Lebanon approved a draft law to end the death penalty completely. This law now awaits a final vote by the full parliament during sessions scheduled for July 15 and 16. If passed, Lebanon would become the first country in the Middle East to officially abolish capital punishment.

**Impact** – This law would replace the death penalty with life imprisonment that includes hard labor, though Lebanon does not currently enforce hard labor punishments. Ending the death penalty is a major step for justice and human rights in Lebanon, especially since about 84 prisoners still face death sentences. The law's approval would mark a clear break from a practice seen as cruel and irreversible.

**What's next step** – Parliament will vote on the draft law soon. If they approve it, Lebanon may also work on removing civilians from military courts that still use the death penalty and could join international agreements that ban capital punishment. Human rights groups hope the law will be improved further to remove harsh punishments like hard labor.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Lebanon is on the verge of becoming the first Middle Eastern country to officially end the death penalty, marking a historic change in its justice system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Liban proche d'abolir la peine de mort
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Liban n'a pas exécuté de peine capitale depuis 2004 et applique actuellement un moratoire non officiel sur la peine de mort. Depuis plusieurs années, les parlementaires libanais et les groupes de défense des droits humains œuvrent pour mettre fin officiellement à cette pratique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 9 juillet 2026, trois commissions parlementaires ont approuvé un projet de loi visant à abolir complètement la peine de mort. Ce texte doit maintenant être voté en séance plénière les 15 et 16 juillet. S'il est adopté, le Liban deviendra le premier pays du Moyen-Orient à supprimer officiellement la peine capitale.

**Impact** – Cette loi remplacerait la peine de mort par la réclusion à perpétuité avec travaux forcés, même si ces derniers ne sont pas appliqués actuellement au Liban. Mettre fin à la peine de mort est une avancée majeure pour la justice et les droits humains au Liban, surtout que près de 84 prisonniers sont encore condamnés à mort. L'adoption de cette loi marquerait une rupture claire avec une pratique jugée cruelle et irréversible.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Parlement doit prochainement voter ce projet de loi. En cas d'adoption, le Liban pourrait aussi retirer les civils de la compétence des tribunaux militaires qui appliquent encore la peine de mort et envisager de rejoindre des accords internationaux contre la peine capitale. Les groupes de défense espèrent aussi que la loi sera améliorée pour supprimer les travaux forcés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Liban est sur le point de devenir le premier pays du Moyen-Orient à abolir officiellement la peine de mort, un changement historique pour son système judiciaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Líbano está cerca de abolir la pena de muerte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Líbano no ha llevado a cabo ejecuciones desde 2004 y mantiene una moratoria no oficial sobre la pena de muerte. Durante años, legisladores y grupos de derechos humanos han trabajado para eliminar formalmente esta práctica en el país.

**Qué pasó** – El 9 de julio de 2026, tres comités parlamentarios aprobaron un proyecto de ley para abolir por completo la pena de muerte. Ahora, el proyecto debe ser votado por la asamblea general del parlamento en sesiones previstas para el 15 y 16 de julio. Si se aprueba, Líbano sería el primer país de Medio Oriente en eliminar oficialmente la pena capital.

**Impacto** – La ley reemplazaría la pena de muerte por cadena perpetua con trabajos forzados, aunque actualmente Líbano no aplica estos trabajos. Terminar con la pena de muerte representa un gran avance para la justicia y los derechos humanos, especialmente porque unos 84 presos aún están condenados a muerte. La aprobación formalizaría un cambio importante y definitivo.

**Próximo paso** – El parlamento votará pronto el proyecto. Si lo aprueba, Líbano podría también sacar a los civiles de la jurisdicción de los tribunales militares que todavía aplican la pena de muerte y considerar unirse a tratados internacionales para abolirla. Los grupos de derechos esperan que la ley se modifique para eliminar los trabajos forzados.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Líbano está a punto de convertirse en el primer país de Medio Oriente que elimina oficialmente la pena de muerte, marcando un cambio histórico en su sistema judicial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an open, ancient courtroom in soft natural tones, where heavy chains and a broken gallows lie discarded in the corner, while a glowing olive branch gently extends from the judge’s bench toward a rising dove silhouette made of light above, symbolizing Lebanon’s parliamentary abolition of the death penalty and the triumph of human rights and justice.

articles.title: France adopts bill legalising assisted dying for adults
articles.summary: French lawmakers on July 15 adopted a bill ​that will create a legal right to assisted dying for adults with incurable illnesses, capping an intense ethical and political debate. The law would enable patients suffering from incurable and life-threatening illnesses to request lethal medication under strict medical supervision. The text will now be presented to the constitutional court, which has up to a month to approve it.
articles.content: French lawmakers on July 15 adopted a bill ​that will create a legal right to assisted dying for adults with incurable illnesses, capping an intense ethical and political debate. The law would enable patients suffering from incurable and life-threatening illnesses to request lethal medication under strict medical supervision. The text will now be presented to the constitutional court, which has up to a month to approve it.
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports the adoption of a significant legal bill granting the right to assisted dying for adults with incurable illnesses in France, representing a major human rights advancement with broad societal impact and detailed context.
articles.personality_title: France approves law allowing assisted dying for adults with incurable illnesses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Until now, people in France with incurable and life-threatening illnesses did not have a legal right to ask for help to end their suffering through assisted dying. This topic has been debated for many years, involving ethical and political discussions about patients' rights and medical care.

**What happened** – On July 15, 2024, French lawmakers passed a new law that gives adults with incurable illnesses the legal right to request assisted dying. This means these patients can ask for medication to end their life, but only with strict medical supervision to ensure safety and rules are followed. The law must now be reviewed by France’s constitutional court, which has up to one month to approve it.

**Impact** – This law is important because it officially recognizes the choice of people suffering from incurable diseases to end their pain in a controlled and legal way. It changes how France supports patients facing the end of life, offering them more control over their own decisions. It also closes a long debate, setting clear rules to protect both patients and doctors.

**What's next step** – The constitutional court will now examine the law to make sure it fits with France’s constitution. If approved, the law will come into effect, and hospitals and doctors will begin following these new rules. This could lead to changes in medical practices and patient care in France.

**One-sentence takeaway** – France has taken a major step by passing a law that allows adults with incurable illnesses to legally choose assisted dying under medical supervision.
articles.personality_title_fr: La France adopte une loi autorisant l’aide à mourir pour les adultes atteints de maladies incurables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Jusqu’à présent, les personnes en France atteintes de maladies incurables et mortelles n’avaient pas le droit légal de demander une aide pour mettre fin à leurs souffrances par une assistance à mourir. Ce sujet a fait l’objet de nombreux débats éthiques et politiques depuis des années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 15 juillet 2024, les députés français ont adopté une nouvelle loi qui donne aux adultes atteints de maladies incurables le droit légal de demander l’aide à mourir. Cela signifie que ces patients peuvent demander un médicament pour mettre fin à leur vie, mais seulement sous une supervision médicale stricte pour garantir la sécurité et le respect des règles. La loi doit maintenant être examinée par le Conseil constitutionnel, qui dispose d’un mois pour l’approuver.

**Impact** – Cette loi est importante car elle reconnaît officiellement le choix des personnes souffrant de maladies incurables de mettre fin à leurs douleurs de manière contrôlée et légale. Elle change la façon dont la France soutient les patients en fin de vie, leur offrant plus de contrôle sur leurs décisions. Elle met aussi fin à un long débat en établissant des règles claires pour protéger les patients et les médecins.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Conseil constitutionnel va maintenant examiner la loi pour vérifier qu’elle respecte la Constitution française. Si elle est approuvée, la loi entrera en vigueur et les hôpitaux ainsi que les médecins devront appliquer ces nouvelles règles. Cela pourrait entraîner des changements dans les pratiques médicales et les soins aux patients en France.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La France franchit une étape importante en adoptant une loi qui permet aux adultes atteints de maladies incurables de choisir légalement l’aide à mourir sous supervision médicale.
articles.personality_title_es: Francia aprueba ley que permite la asistencia para morir a adultos con enfermedades incurables
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hasta ahora, las personas en Francia con enfermedades incurables y mortales no tenían el derecho legal de pedir ayuda para terminar con su sufrimiento mediante la asistencia para morir. Este tema ha sido objeto de debates éticos y políticos durante muchos años.

**Qué pasó** – El 15 de julio de 2024, los legisladores franceses aprobaron una nueva ley que otorga a los adultos con enfermedades incurables el derecho legal a solicitar la asistencia para morir. Esto significa que estos pacientes pueden pedir medicamentos para terminar con su vida, pero solo bajo estricta supervisión médica para asegurar la seguridad y el cumplimiento de las normas. La ley ahora será revisada por el tribunal constitucional de Francia, que tiene hasta un mes para aprobarla.

**Impacto** – Esta ley es importante porque reconoce oficialmente la elección de las personas que sufren enfermedades incurables de terminar con su dolor de forma controlada y legal. Cambia la forma en que Francia apoya a los pacientes en el final de la vida, ofreciéndoles más control sobre sus propias decisiones. También pone fin a un largo debate, estableciendo reglas claras para proteger a pacientes y médicos.

**Próximo paso** – El tribunal constitucional revisará la ley para asegurarse de que cumpla con la constitución de Francia. Si se aprueba, la ley entrará en vigor y los hospitales y médicos comenzarán a seguir estas nuevas reglas. Esto podría llevar a cambios en las prácticas médicas y en el cuidado de los pacientes en Francia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Francia da un paso importante al aprobar una ley que permite a adultos con enfermedades incurables elegir legalmente la asistencia para morir bajo supervisión médica.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly lit scene of two hands gently passing a delicate, glowing vial of medicine over a wooden table, surrounded by warm, muted earth tones and subtle abstract shapes suggesting care, hope, and dignity in the face of illness.

articles.title: Peacekeepers step up support as families return to south Lebanon
articles.summary: With violence in south Lebanon significantly decreased since mid-June, UN peacekeepers there have returned to core functions such as protecting humanitarian aid and restoring critical infrastructure as families continue to return home.
articles.content:
“The welcome reduction in violence allows our peacekeepers to do more of what we are here to do,” UNIFIL spokesperson Kandice Ardiel said in a press release on Thursday.

The UN Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL)’s mandate is to enforce Security Council Resolution 1701 – which set the terms for ending the fighting between Hezbollah and Israeli in 2006 – by monitoring hostilities, supporting Lebanese armed forces against Hezbollah militants, ensuring humanitarian access and securing Lebanon’s border along the Blue Line of separation from Israel.

A broken peace

The 2006 resolution aimed to create lasting peace between Israel and Hezbollah and disarm the latter. Since 8 October 2023, UN Secretary-General António Guterres has accused both sides of violating the resolution.

Israel and the Lebanese Government in Beirut have engaged in peace talks since June. UNIFIL has recorded dozens of projectiles over the past few days, which still represents a relative decrease in hostilities.

Meanwhile, as many families have returned to their homes in south Lebanon only to find their villages destroyed, UNIFIL peacekeepers have worked to facilitate humanitarian assistance by ensuring safe passage for aid convoys and help restore essential services such as healthcare and water.

“As people have begun to return to their villages, a lot of work remains to be done,” Ms. Ardiel said.

Tweet URL

Recent work on the ground

In the past few weeks, UNIFIL has provided medical equipment and emergency room supplies to Tibnin Governmental Hospital, strengthening critical healthcare infrastructure.

UNIFIL peacekeepers have facilitated the movement of civilian workers, technical teams, humanitarian convoys and mobile medical clinics carrying out repairs and services related to water networks, electricity systems, telecommunications infrastructure and healthcare delivery.

Through these efforts, UNIFIL has helped repair and maintain water infrastructure in dozens of towns and villages in south Lebanon in recent weeks.

Dealing with unexploded ordnance

Another way UNIFIL protects civilians is by disarming the unexploded ordnances which now litter southern Lebanon and threaten local communities.

Since the most recent escalation in March as the US and Israel began bombing Iran and Hezbollah joined the fight in support of Tehran, UNIFIL peacekeepers have neutralized dozens of unexploded ordnances, improvised explosive devices and crashed drones.

On Wednesday, the Lebanese Armed Forces successfully neutralized several ordnances discovered by UNIFIL peacekeepers, according to UN spokesperson Stéphane Dujarric.

“Explosive hazards remain one of the most serious challenges facing returning communities,” UNIFIL wrote in a press release.

UNIFIL continues to urge all parties to comply with Resolution 1701: “Peacekeepers continue to work alongside local authorities and communities to support recovery, reduce risks and contribute to long-term stability in south Lebanon,” said the mission.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on UN peacekeepers enabling families to safely return to south Lebanon by reducing violence, facilitating humanitarian aid, restoring critical infrastructure, and neutralizing explosive hazards. This has a broad positive impact on civilian safety and recovery in a conflict-affected region, with clear details on the peacekeeping efforts and their tangible benefits.
articles.personality_title: UN peacekeepers help families return safely to south Lebanon
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – South Lebanon has faced violence and destruction due to conflict between Israel and Hezbollah. Since mid-June, violence has decreased, allowing families to come back to their homes.

**What happened** – The United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) has increased its efforts to protect aid, fix important services like water and healthcare, and clear dangerous unexploded bombs. They have helped hospitals get medical supplies and supported workers repairing damaged infrastructure.

**Impact** – These actions make it safer for families to live in their villages again and help communities recover from damage. Removing unexploded bombs prevents accidents and saves lives. This work supports peace talks and reduces risks for people returning home.

**What's next step** – UNIFIL will keep working with local authorities to maintain peace, support rebuilding, and encourage all parties to follow the peace agreement known as Resolution 1701.

**One-sentence takeaway** – UN peacekeepers are making south Lebanon safer and helping families rebuild by reducing violence, restoring services, and removing dangerous explosives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les Casques bleus facilitent le retour des familles dans le sud du Liban
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le sud du Liban a été marqué par des violences liées au conflit entre Israël et le Hezbollah. Depuis la mi-juin, ces violences ont diminué, permettant aux familles de revenir chez elles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La Force intérimaire des Nations unies au Liban (FINUL) a renforcé ses actions pour protéger l’aide humanitaire, réparer des services essentiels comme l’eau et la santé, et neutraliser les bombes non explosées. Elle a aidé les hôpitaux à recevoir du matériel médical et soutenu les équipes réparant les infrastructures endommagées.

**Impact** – Ces efforts rendent la vie plus sûre pour les familles et aident les communautés à se reconstruire. En enlevant les bombes non explosées, elles préviennent des accidents et sauvent des vies. Ce travail soutient aussi les négociations de paix et réduit les risques pour les habitants.

**Prochaine étape** – La FINUL continuera de collaborer avec les autorités locales pour maintenir la paix, accompagner la reconstruction et encourager le respect de la résolution 1701.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les Casques bleus rendent le sud du Liban plus sûr et aident les familles à reconstruire en réduisant les violences, en restaurant les services et en éliminant les explosifs dangereux.
articles.personality_title_es: Los cascos azules ayudan a las familias a regresar con seguridad al sur del Líbano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sur del Líbano ha sufrido violencia por el conflicto entre Israel y Hezbolá. Desde mediados de junio, la violencia ha disminuido, lo que permite que las familias regresen a sus hogares.

**Qué pasó** – La Fuerza Interina de las Naciones Unidas en el Líbano (FINUL) ha intensificado sus esfuerzos para proteger la ayuda humanitaria, reparar servicios importantes como el agua y la salud, y desactivar bombas sin explotar. Han ayudado a hospitales con suministros médicos y apoyado a los equipos que reparan infraestructuras dañadas.

**Impacto** – Estas acciones hacen que sea más seguro para las familias vivir en sus pueblos y ayudan a las comunidades a recuperarse. Quitar las bombas sin explotar previene accidentes y salva vidas. Este trabajo también apoya las conversaciones de paz y reduce los riesgos para las personas que regresan.

**Próximo paso** – FINUL seguirá trabajando con las autoridades locales para mantener la paz, apoyar la reconstrucción y animar a todas las partes a respetar la resolución 1701.

**Frase clave** – Los cascos azules están haciendo el sur del Líbano más seguro y ayudando a las familias a reconstruir al reducir la violencia, restaurar servicios y eliminar explosivos peligrosos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a peaceful southern Lebanese village at dawn, with symbolic white dove-shaped peacekeepers gently guiding a convoy of aid trucks and mobile clinics along a winding road past repaired water pipes and power lines, while soft silhouettes of villagers return to partially restored homes amid cleared fields dotted with safely disarmed unexploded ordnance marked by neutralized, inactive devices blending into the landscape.

articles.title: Gaza farmers battle to rebuild as WFP warns of West Bank settler violence
articles.summary: The UN is supporting farmers in Gaza who are struggling to rebuild their smallholdings in areas devastated by the Hamas-Israel war.
articles.content:
These growers include Taysir Dahdouh, whose farm in the Zeytun neighbourhood, east of Gaza City, is a little smaller than a football pitch.

His land was once covered with greenhouses, but they’ve been destroyed or lost during the conflict that erupted on 7 October 2023. Today, he needs tools, seeds, fertilizer and water to grow the cucumbers and tomatoes he used to.

UN News

200 new homes

Alessandro Mrakic, Head of the Gaza Office for the UN Development Programme (UNDP), told UN News’s correspondent in the enclave that families who had once lived in one heavily bombed area had had to move multiple times before returning.

“We have provided 200 relief housing units to provide shelter to the families who came back and started, as you see behind me, agriculture – started producing eggplants, tomatoes, molokhia, among others,” he said.

The UN’s Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) said on Thursday it has scaled up its cash assistance programme to help around 1,500 Palestinian farmers cultivate land across Gaza during the 2026 planting season, enough to produce fresh vegetables for more than 100,000 people.

Not enough space

But FAO warned that farmers “are squeezed into rapidly shrinking space” amid ongoing Israeli military activity, and it is calling for access to land, sea and production inputs including seeds, fertilizers, irrigation equipment and fishing gear.

Elsewhere, Deputy Special Coordinator for the peace process Ramiz Alakbarov welcomed new commitments worth almost $58 million from eight Member States to the UN Horizon Fund, and called on further partners to join the initiative.

The World Food Programme (WFP) said people forced to move within Gaza risk losing access to basic services and called for safe access to reach those in need; despite the obstacles, it and its partners reached more than a quarter of a million people across 36 distribution sites in the first 12 days of this month.

UN News

Summer respite

Humanitarian partners also distributed more than 5,440 educational kits to support around 217,600 children during summer learning activities.

Turning to the West Bank, a high-level delegation led by aid coordination office OCHA visited Deir Nidham village in the Ramallah governorate on Wednesday, meeting Palestinian families affected by settler violence and the expansion of settlement outposts.

Settler attacks accounted for around 55 per cent of all Palestinian injuries in the West Bank so far during 2026.

Guterres voices alarm

UN Spokesperson Stéphane Dujarric said the Secretary-General is “deeply alarmed” that Israeli authorities have granted city status to Givat Ze’ev, a settlement northwest of Jerusalem, adding that the designation “does not alter the city’s legal status under international law as part of the Occupied Palestinian Territory.”

All Israeli settlements in the occupied West Bank, including East Jerusalem, are illegal under international law, Mr. Dujarric reiterated, recalling the International Court of Justice’s advisory opinion of July 2024.

He said settlements remain a major obstacle to a two-State solution, and renewed the Secretary-General’s call on Israel to halt all settlement expansion.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the UN's tangible support to Gaza farmers rebuilding their livelihoods after conflict, including provision of housing and agricultural assistance benefiting thousands, which has broad humanitarian significance. It also highlights ongoing challenges and calls for peaceful solutions, providing sufficient context and focusing on a meaningful humanitarian issue affecting many people.
articles.personality_title: UN helps Gaza farmers rebuild after conflict with new homes and farming support
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Farmers in Gaza lost much of their land and equipment during the war between Hamas and Israel that began in October 2023. Many families had to move several times because of heavy bombing.

**What happened** – The United Nations has stepped in to help these farmers recover. They provided 200 new homes for families returning to their land and are helping about 1,500 farmers with money to buy seeds, tools, and fertilizer for the 2026 planting season. This support will help grow fresh vegetables like cucumbers, tomatoes, and eggplants for over 100,000 people.

**Impact** – This assistance means farmers can start growing food again, which is important for their income and for feeding local communities. Even though space for farming is getting smaller because of ongoing military activity, the UN’s help is making a real difference in rebuilding lives and food supplies.

**What's next step** – The UN and other groups will keep working to provide safe access to land and resources for farmers. They also want more countries to join their efforts by funding projects that support peace and development. Meanwhile, attention is also focused on reducing violence in the West Bank to protect families there.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s housing and farming support is helping Gaza farmers rebuild their livelihoods and grow food again despite ongoing challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU aide les agriculteurs de Gaza à reconstruire avec de nouveaux logements et un soutien agricole
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les agriculteurs de Gaza ont perdu une grande partie de leurs terres et équipements lors de la guerre entre le Hamas et Israël commencée en octobre 2023. De nombreuses familles ont dû déménager plusieurs fois à cause des bombardements.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Organisation des Nations Unies est intervenue pour aider ces agriculteurs à se relever. Elle a fourni 200 nouveaux logements aux familles retournant sur leurs terres et aide environ 1 500 agriculteurs avec de l’argent pour acheter des semences, des outils et des engrais pour la saison de plantation 2026. Ce soutien permettra de cultiver des légumes frais comme des concombres, des tomates et des aubergines pour plus de 100 000 personnes.

**Impact** – Cette aide permet aux agriculteurs de recommencer à produire de la nourriture, ce qui est important pour leurs revenus et pour nourrir les communautés locales. Même si l’espace agricole se réduit à cause des activités militaires, l’aide de l’ONU fait une réelle différence pour reconstruire des vies et l’approvisionnement alimentaire.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU et d’autres organisations continueront à travailler pour garantir un accès sécurisé aux terres et aux ressources agricoles. Elles souhaitent aussi que davantage de pays participent financièrement aux projets soutenant la paix et le développement. Parallèlement, l’attention est portée sur la réduction des violences en Cisjordanie pour protéger les familles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le soutien de l’ONU en logements et agriculture aide les agriculteurs de Gaza à reconstruire leur vie et à cultiver à nouveau malgré les difficultés.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU ayuda a agricultores de Gaza a reconstruir con nuevas viviendas y apoyo agrícola
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los agricultores en Gaza perdieron gran parte de sus tierras y herramientas durante la guerra entre Hamás e Israel que comenzó en octubre de 2023. Muchas familias tuvieron que mudarse varias veces debido a los bombardeos intensos.

**Qué pasó** – Las Naciones Unidas han intervenido para ayudar a estos agricultores a recuperarse. Proporcionaron 200 nuevas viviendas para las familias que regresan a sus tierras y están ayudando a unos 1,500 agricultores con dinero para comprar semillas, herramientas y fertilizantes para la temporada de siembra de 2026. Este apoyo ayudará a cultivar verduras frescas como pepinos, tomates y berenjenas para más de 100,000 personas.

**Impacto** – Esta ayuda significa que los agricultores pueden volver a cultivar alimentos, lo que es importante para sus ingresos y para alimentar a las comunidades locales. Aunque el espacio para cultivar se está reduciendo debido a la actividad militar, la ayuda de la ONU está marcando una diferencia real en la reconstrucción de vidas y el suministro de alimentos.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU y otros grupos seguirán trabajando para proporcionar acceso seguro a las tierras y recursos para los agricultores. También quieren que más países se unan a sus esfuerzos financiando proyectos que apoyen la paz y el desarrollo. Mientras tanto, se presta atención a reducir la violencia en Cisjordania para proteger a las familias.

**Resumen en una frase** – El apoyo de la ONU con viviendas y agricultura está ayudando a los agricultores de Gaza a reconstruir sus medios de vida y volver a cultivar alimentos a pesar de los desafíos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a small, lush farm plot with vibrant rows of cucumbers, tomatoes, and eggplants growing amid simple greenhouses made of light wooden frames and translucent fabric, set against a softly glowing Gaza city skyline at dusk, with a few scattered relief housing units nearby and a gentle hand watering the plants symbolizing care and renewal.
